description: Namespace: context. Tag terms that name alternative school justice adjacent context as a circumstance, barrier, school condition, or intervention context. DO NOT treat adjacent discipline, safety, gangs, or violence language as incarceration; keep direct justice involvement separate.
rationale: Alternative school, court school, detention education, reentry settings; only...

description: Namespace: context. Tag terms that name direct attendance absence terms as a circumstance, barrier, school condition, or intervention context. DO NOT infer attendance issues from generic engagement, motivation, belonging, or hardship unless attendance/absence is explicit or mechanically tied.
rationale: Absence, attendance, chronic absence, missed school, absenteeism. Direct anch...

description: Namespace: context. Tag terms that name discipline exclusion absence as a circumstance, barrier, school condition, or intervention context. DO NOT infer attendance issues from generic engagement, motivation, belonging, or hardship unless attendance/absence is explicit or mechanically tied.
rationale: Suspension, exclusion, out-of-class time; attendance-adjacent but not incarce...

description: Namespace: context. Tag terms that name mentoring protective support as a circumstance, barrier, school condition, or intervention context. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Mentorship/trusted adult supports for justice-adjacent or violence-exposed yo...

description: Namespace: context. Tag terms that name rural broadband technology access as a circumstance, barrier, school condition, or intervention context. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Connectivity, broadband, devices in rural context; not all tech access is rural.

description: Namespace: context. Tag terms that name rural distance access barrier as a circumstance, barrier, school condition, or intervention context. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Distance, remote access, long travel, rural scarcity; high funder relevance.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around independence, initiative, reliability, ownership, responsibility, or follow-through as durable student habits. DO NOT tag generic choice, personalization, or student-voice language unless the essay explicitly emphasizes accountability, self-management, or dependable action.
rationale: This should catch work-habit language about responsibility, initiative, and follow-through, not broad autonomy or personalization language on its own.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around teaching students what AI is, how generative AI or LLM tools work, how to use AI responsibly, and how to evaluate AI-generated content critically. DO NOT tag generic coding, robotics, digital citizenship, privacy, bias, algorithm, academic integrity, or device language unless AI, generative AI, chatbots, LLMs, prompting, or named AI tools are explicit or strongly central.
rationale: Direct AI literacy/responsible-use framing only. Generic digital citizenship, privacy, algorithms, or academic integrity are adjacent signals but not positive AI anchors without explicit AI context.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as a substantial build-out, specialized initiative, or multi-part program rather than a modest baseline purchase. DO NOT tag starter, essential, or bare-minimum terms; basic, necessities, and refill are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary clearly signals a large programmatic scope.
rationale: Media-lab builds and academy-style STEM initiatives should score high; starter supply kits and pantry refills should score low. This should rise in non-crisis periods and in schools pursuing visible program growth.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that cue a narrated vignette, classroom moment, or scene-setting observation rather than a bare abstract summary. Boundary: unlike sensory_detail, this category requires a story-like moment or observed scene; unlike causal_specificity, it does not require an explicit material-to-outcome chain. DO NOT tag isolated tactile words or generic mechanism words such as sensory or outcome unless the term helps build a vignette.
rationale: Anecdotal read-aloud and morning-routine essays should score high; terse equipment requests and standards-justified intervention essays should score low. This should stay moderate overall, with slightly higher prevalence in early childhood and elementary literacy writing.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around completing deliverables, producing work products, executing multi-step projects, or preparing artifacts for practical use, portfolios, clients, or presentation. DO NOT tag generic creativity or public-audience language unless the essay explicitly emphasizes workflow, completion, execution, or concrete outputs.
rationale: This should catch project execution and concrete output language—artifacts, deliverables, portfolios, prototypes, and workflow—rather than general creativity or public-audience framing alone.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around real-world application, public audience, publication, performance, or learning that reaches beyond the classroom bubble. DO NOT tag generic future-readiness or vague career terms alone; career and pathway are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary points to authentic output or external audience.
rationale: Student newspaper, podcast, and exhibition projects should score high; basic workbook replenishment and test-prep requests should score low. This should rise in spring showcase seasons and in project-based learning pushes.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that describe removing a concrete obstacle blocking ordinary participation, access, focus, comfort, or routine classroom functioning. Treat this as a broad background/access signal, not as a standalone topical insight. DO NOT tag generic opportunity, enrichment, future-readiness, robotics, studio, showcase, or field-trip language unless the essay centers on a blocked baseline condition.
rationale: Broad substrate signal for blocked baseline participation. Useful as a background/context tag and cross-tab, but should not by itself become a repeated top-line insight.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that mark the request as aiming for a minimal workable baseline rather than a premium upgrade or ambitious expansion. DO NOT tag aspirational build-out or showcase language; innovation, studio, and competition terms are NOT applicable unless the essay explicitly stresses first getting to a bare minimum.
rationale: Starter supply kits and first-baseline reading sets should score high; full media-lab expansions and specialized theater builds should score low. This should rise in inflation periods and in lower-cost supply requests.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around cultural reflection, identity affirmation, mirrors, voice, heritage, or students seeing themselves represented. This category is about relational belonging, affirmation, and being seen, not primarily the curriculum-materials dimension of representation. Boundary: unlike inclusion_accommodation_framing, this category is about representation and voice, not disability logistics, translation, or participation access. DO NOT tag assistive, bilingual-access, or adapted-instruction terms unless the term itself signals identity reflection.
rationale: Heritage-book collections and student-storytelling anthologies should score high; speech-language devices and translated directions should score low. This should rise with culturally responsive literacy waves and identity-focused curriculum periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that portray students through capability, pride, dignity, voice, or strength even when need is present. DO NOT tag vocabulary that foregrounds embarrassment, exposure, or fragility as the main student portrait; crisis, trauma, or helplessness terms are NOT applicable unless they are paired with an overt strength-first framing.
rationale: Student-publication projects and arts showcases should score high; emergency pantry and hygiene-closet projects should score low. The signal should be fairly stable, with modest rises in arts, leadership, and student-voice waves.

description: Namespace: framing. Tag terms that frame need through career exploration pathways; require a contrastive rhetorical or contextual signal. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Career exploration, pathway exposure, job awareness, career clusters.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around helping students imagine, explore, or understand jobs, professions, industries, or future occupational roles. DO NOT tag vague future-readiness language unless the essay explicitly references careers or workplaces.
rationale: This is the clearest direct workforce-facing category and should catch explicit occupational imagination or exposure.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as helping students recover lost ground, close gaps, rebuild prerequisite academic skills, or return to expected proficiency. DO NOT tag forward-looking enrichment, coding, robotics, maker, or showcase language unless the essay explicitly stresses academic catch-up/remediation.
rationale: Reading-intervention kits and tutoring projects should score high; coding-club expansion, robotics, and maker build-outs should score low unless explicitly about remediation.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that make the mechanism explicit by linking materials, activities, and outcomes in a clear use-to-result chain. Boundary: unlike sensory_detail, this category does not require perceptual vocabulary; unlike anecdote_scene_setting, it does not require a story moment. DO NOT tag generic uplift words such as confidence, curiosity, or success language unless the term is part of an explicit mechanism or outcome chain.
rationale: Phonics tool requests and structured science-kit proposals should score high; broad wish-list essays and purely atmospheric classroom redesigns should score low. This should be relatively stable, with somewhat higher prevalence in intervention and special-education writing.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame need as persistent background under-resourcing rather than a discrete disruption, launch, or recovery cycle. DO NOT tag event-shaped crisis, named disaster, one-time repair, or ambitious expansion language unless the essay mainly stresses steady insufficiency.
rationale: Persistent under-resourcing signal. Useful background/context tag; avoid repeatedly converting ordinary scarcity into top-line insight unless the slice-specific mechanism differs.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around coding, sequencing, logic, debugging, algorithms, or computational thinking as skill-building, but without explicit AI language. DO NOT tag essays that explicitly center AI, chatbots, LLMs, or generative tools; those belong in the AI categories instead.
rationale: This is an intentional non-AI adjacency tag that can surface classrooms already working in code- and logic-based spaces that may be ripe for later AI outreach.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around building interpersonal effectiveness such as cooperation, listening, discussion norms, conflict navigation, or working productively with others. DO NOT tag essays that merely use group work, shared tables, or collaborative formats unless the essay explicitly emphasizes interpersonal skill development.
rationale: This is the interpersonal-skill version of collaboration and should fire when the essay stresses empathy, listening, dialogue, or teamwork habits rather than collaborative classroom structure alone.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around teamwork, peer interaction, dialogue, shared production, or cooperative learning structures rather than solitary individualized work. DO NOT tag regulation, calm-space, subgroup accommodation, or generic group seating unless the essay explicitly emphasizes collaborative learning.
rationale: Collaboration as pedagogy or production structure; do not absorb regulation spaces or generic group furniture.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around the feel of the room—coziness, ambiance, warmth, softness, or welcoming atmosphere—rather than a direct instructional mechanism. DO NOT tag explicit sensory-regulation or causal-mechanism language; self regulate, used for, and benchmark are NOT applicable unless the term is mainly about atmosphere.
rationale: Reading-nook rugs, lamp-lighting, and classroom ambiance projects should score high; phonics intervention kits and benchmark-prep materials should score low. This should be fairly stable, with mild rises in literacy-corner redesign trends.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as restocking or repeatedly replacing items that get used up, run out, are depleted, eaten, distributed, or consumed. DO NOT tag durable build-out, fixed installation, furniture, lab stations, or long-term program infrastructure unless the request is mainly about replenishment.
rationale: Recurring depletion/restock signal. Keep separate from durable infrastructure and avoid promoting as a fresh insight unless timing or slice-specific replenishment behavior matters.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that stress a narrow timing window, closing opportunity, or fixed deadline rather than ordinary urgency alone. DO NOT tag broad urgent-now language without a time window; urgent, immediate, and pressing are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary signals a closing window or deadline.
rationale: Performance-season costume requests and before-testing intervention asks should score high; evergreen class-library refreshes should score low. This should peak in testing windows and end-of-year performance periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that argue students deserve equal treatment, a fair chance, or parity with peers rather than emphasizing novelty or joy. DO NOT tag generic pride or celebration language; showcase, wonder, and creativity terms are NOT applicable unless the appeal explicitly invokes fairness or deserved access.
rationale: Resource-parity requests and equity-driven book access projects should score high; theater costume builds and robotics competitions should score low. This should rise in equity-focused years and in periods of stronger public discourse around educational fairness.

description: Namespace: framing. Tag terms that frame need through disability accommodation access; require a contrastive rhetorical or contextual signal. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit. Boundary: keep disability/accommodation logistics separate from identity affirmation, language identity, and general inclusion rhetoric.
rationale: Accessibility/accommodation language for disability participation.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as building, installing, or acquiring durable classroom infrastructure meant to last across many cycles of use. DO NOT tag refill, one-time depletion, generic storage for consumables, or routine restocking unless the essay is mainly about lasting build-out.
rationale: Durable build-out signal. Keep distinct from consumables and generic storage; use as an infrastructure descriptor unless paired with a distinctive domain mechanism.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that invite the donor to feel with students through compassion, care, comfort, or human closeness rather than through institutional logic or metrics. DO NOT tag accountability or compliance language; standards, benchmark, and evidence-based terms are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary itself asks for emotional identification.
rationale: Care-closet essays and personal classroom narratives should score high; rubric-driven intervention requests should score low. This should rise in winter hardship appeals and in highly personalized teacher narratives.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame need as event-shaped: disruption, crisis, interruption, return, rebuilding, or recovery after a named or nameable shock. DO NOT tag terms that only describe steady under-resourcing or household hardship without an event shape; words like underfunded, pantry, or shortage are NOT applicable unless they point to disruption or recovery.
rationale: COVID-recovery device requests and post-storm classroom rebuild projects should score high; steady pantry restock and robotics-lab launch projects should score low. This signal should spike in 2020-2021 and in quarters following named disasters or reopening periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that anchor the request to a named event, newsworthy disruption, disaster, or public crisis rather than to ordinary classroom chronology. DO NOT tag generic urgent or recovery language without a named event context; urgent, immediate, and rebuilding are NOT applicable unless they point to a specific event or disaster frame.
rationale: Post-hurricane classroom rebuilds and COVID-response device projects should score high; routine pantry restocks and ordinary replacement requests should score low. This should spike sharply around major disruptions and then recede.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that emphasize tactile, manipulative, lab-based, maker, or embodied academic learning rather than primarily text-based, screen-based, comfort, or regulation support. DO NOT tag sensory-regulation vocabulary such as calm corners, weighted items, cozy seating, or quiet spaces unless the term refers to academic hands-on learning.
rationale: Science lab kits, math manipulatives, and maker projects should score high; regulation and comfort materials should not be pulled into hands-on learning.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around making, composing, designing, performing, or producing original student work rather than only consuming or practicing. DO NOT tag reading-level repair or passive reception terms; decodable, intervention, and listening-only vocabulary are NOT applicable unless students are creating something.
rationale: Theater set builds, student publishing projects, and visual-arts requests should score high; phonics recovery kits and passive listening stations should score low. This should rise in arts-rich quarters and exhibition seasons.

description: Namespace: framing. Tag terms that frame need through hunger, food insecurity, or food access as a concrete barrier to participation/readiness. DO NOT collapse hunger relief, nutrition instruction, cooking, agriculture, and food systems unless the term explicitly belongs to hunger or food access.
rationale: Direct hunger/food access signal. Keep distinct from nutrition education, culinary life skills, and agriculture/food systems learning.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around building language, listening, speaking, early literacy, vocabulary, comprehension, or expressive communication as durable capability. DO NOT tag generic book or reading requests unless the essay explicitly emphasizes communication or literacy development.
rationale: This captures broad communication-capacity building, especially in early childhood and elementary projects, rather than narrow remediation alone.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as preparing students for upcoming stages, future opportunities, or longer-horizon readiness beyond the immediate classroom moment. DO NOT tag terms driven by immediate relief, emergency response, or same-week urgency; pantry, replacement, and crisis vocabulary is NOT applicable unless it explicitly points to future preparation.
rationale: Career-tech equipment requests and college-readiness projects should score high; emergency snack pantry and post-storm rebuild requests should score low. The signal should rise in spring transition seasons and in CTE expansion periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that orient the essay toward thanking, appreciation, and donor acknowledgment rather than detailing mission or mechanism. DO NOT tag modest asking or partnership language alone; please, sponsor, and join us are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary explicitly centers gratitude.
rationale: Teacher-thank-you closing sections and heartfelt supply appeals should score high; terse standards-based intervention requests should score low. This should be fairly stable, with slightly higher rates in smaller personal classroom appeals.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame materials as intentionally traveling beyond the classroom into homes, caregivers, or family co-learning routines. DO NOT tag direct household hardship terms like pantry or hygiene spillover; groceries, winter coat, and laundry are NOT applicable unless the term points to take-home learning use rather than hardship relief.
rationale: Take-home literacy bags and bilingual family reading kits should score high; in-class only lab carts and pantry snack projects should score low. This should rise around summer-learning pushes and family-engagement seasons.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that describe home-to-school transfer of concrete basic-needs strain such as hunger, hygiene, clothing, laundry, sleep, or food insecurity affecting classroom participation. DO NOT tag structural equity or low-income terms without concrete household hardship; DO NOT tag nutrition, cooking, agriculture, or food systems instruction.
rationale: Concrete household hardship spilling into school participation. Broad background signal; should be separated from nutrition/cooking/agriculture instruction.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that position the request as a modest ask, appeal, or earnest plea rather than a confident investment pitch. DO NOT tag gratitude or partnership language on its own; thank you, collaboration, and sponsor are NOT applicable unless the tone is clearly supplicatory.
rationale: Small classroom wish-list essays and modest supply appeals should score high; entrepreneurship labs pitched as investments should score low. This should be relatively stable, with slight rises in teacher-personalized appeals.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around access logistics for disability, language access, adapted participation, or classroom accommodation. DO NOT tag cultural representation or belonging language whose main point is identity reflection or voice; heritage, mirror books, and student voice terms are NOT applicable unless the essay centers on participation logistics or accommodation.
rationale: Adaptive-seating, speech-language, and bilingual-access projects should score high; heritage-book collections and student-storytelling anthologies should score low. The signal should strengthen in inclusion-focused years and around multilingual-access pushes.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame learning as questioning, investigating, experimenting, or open-ended discovery rather than routine drill or repetition. Boundary: wonder in a question, investigation, or hypothesis-building context belongs here; wonder used mainly as delight, magic, or inspiration belongs more naturally in joy_possibility_appeal. DO NOT tag remediation or benchmark language; intervention, decodable, and proficiency terms are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary clearly signals inquiry.
rationale: Science-investigation kits and project-based STEM requests should score high; phonics remediation packets and test-prep binders should score low. This should rise in makerspace and STEM push periods after the pandemic recovery trough.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that signal auditability, stewardship, measurable use, or responsible management rather than emotional intimacy. DO NOT tag empathy or gratitude language; compassion, heart, and thankful are NOT applicable unless the term itself stresses accountability or stewardship.
rationale: Measurable intervention programs and tightly justified tech requests should score high; heartfelt care-closet essays should score low. This should rise in data-heavy eras and in projects framed for institutional credibility.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that point to background supports that make participation possible without being the main visible instructional object. DO NOT tag tangible feature items or showcase equipment; robot, ukulele, and bookshelf are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary itself signals behind-the-scenes enabling support.
rationale: Charging-cart, software-license, and translation-tool projects should score high; theater-set lumber and robotics kits should score low. This should grow in device-heavy and multilingual-access periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that persuade through delight, inspiration, wonder, celebration, or excitement about what becomes possible. DO NOT tag prevention or harm-avoidance language, and DO NOT tag concrete opportunity-opening language unless the persuasive force is emotional uplift rather than access to a new pathway.
rationale: Arts showcase requests, playground builds, and imaginative literacy projects should score high; attendance-intervention and pantry essays should score low. This should rebound in post-crisis periods and in arts-rich seasons.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that emphasize wide variation in readiness, language, disability, pace, or learning profile within the same room or service setting. DO NOT tag a single named subgroup unless the vocabulary signals multiple profiles or differentiation complexity; newcomers or autistic alone are NOT applicable unless the term points to mixed-needs variation.
rationale: Mixed-readiness classroom library and differentiated station projects should score high; subgroup-targeted sensory kits for one population should score low. This should grow with heterogeneous classroom language and inclusion expansion.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that persuade by emphasizing what will be prevented, avoided, or not lost if the project is funded: missed instruction, exclusion, hunger, anxiety, or falling behind. DO NOT tag upbeat possibility or celebration language whose main force is gain or excitement; showcase, joy, and discovery terms are NOT applicable unless the essay is primarily preventing harm.
rationale: Attendance, intervention, and care-closet projects should score high; theater-set builds and student-publication launches should score low. This framing should intensify during disruption, inflation, and winter hardship periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that emphasize upkeep, routine replacement, or ongoing maintenance of existing materials rather than introducing something new. If the replacement is framed as routine refresh, lifecycle upkeep, or scheduled renewal, prefer this category; if the item is described as broken, damaged, or failing now, prefer triage_repair. DO NOT tag launch, pilot, or first-time program language; makerspace, inaugural, and startup terms are NOT applicable unless the request is clearly about maintaining an existing setup.
rationale: Replacement Chromebook carts and worn-library-furniture refreshes should score high; first-time podcast studios and new debate teams should score low. This framing should be steadier over time, with small rises after heavy-use pandemic years.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that justify the request by invoking requirements, standards, service obligations, legal accommodations, or formal compliance language. Boundary: unlike readiness_to_learn, this category describes justification mode rather than student condition. DO NOT tag calm, hunger, focus, or comfort terms unless the vocabulary explicitly points to a mandate or requirement.
rationale: Assessment-prep materials and IEP-aligned accommodation requests should score high; snack carts and calm-corner projects should score low. This should rise around standards-heavy eras, testing seasons, and post-ESSA accountability language.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around a culminating performance, showcase, graduation, capstone, or final product rather than routine daily instruction. DO NOT tag ordinary public-audience or seasonal terms unless the vocabulary specifically marks culmination or a final milestone.
rationale: Senior capstone displays and spring concert projects should score high; everyday reading-corner replenishment should score low. This should rise in spring and end-of-year quarters.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around gardens, outdoor classrooms, ecology, environmental stewardship, composting, sustainability, or learning in and through the natural environment. DO NOT tag generic enrichment or expansion language unless the essay explicitly centers outdoor or environmental learning.
rationale: Garden builds, outdoor science projects, and sustainability-focused requests should score high; generic enrichment, indoor lab, and replacement-supply requests should score low. This makes the outdoor and environmental cluster visible rather than absorbing it into broader opportunity framing.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that indicate a first-time launch, debut, pilot, or startup rather than maintaining or repairing something already in place. DO NOT tag upkeep or replacement language; repair, refresh, and worn out are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary clearly signals a new launch.
rationale: First-time podcast studios and inaugural gardening clubs should score high; broken-device replacements and worn-furniture refreshes should score low. This should be more common in post-crisis expansion periods than in disruption-heavy quarters.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around number sense, math fluency, computation, fractions, place value, manipulatives, or other math-specific skill-building. DO NOT tag generic hands-on or intervention language unless the essay explicitly centers numeracy or math development.
rationale: Math-manipulative projects, number-sense interventions, and fluency-building requests should score high; literacy-focused, generic hands-on, and non-academic hardship requests should score low. This adds a clear math counterpart to the literacy and language skill lane.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that rely on witnessed classroom observation, teacher testimony, or direct lived evidence rather than formal metrics or external justification. DO NOT tag standards, benchmark, or research terms unless the vocabulary itself signals witnessing or testimony.
rationale: Teacher-witness essays about attention, reading habits, or classroom routines should score high; data-heavy intervention requests should score low. This should be fairly stable, with somewhat higher use in elementary and relationship-centered essays.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as opening a new possibility, pathway, experience, or aspirational horizon rather than fixing a blocked baseline condition. This category is about opening a concrete path or activity, not primarily about delight or wonder. DO NOT tag terms centered on access repair, regulation, replacement, or pantry-style relief; headphones, breakfast, and replacement language are NOT applicable unless the essay is clearly about expansion.
rationale: Robotics kits, media labs, and greenhouse projects should score high; hygiene closets and replacement Chromebook requests should score low. This signal should strengthen in non-crisis periods and in STEM or arts push cycles after the sharp pandemic recovery years.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that position donors as partners, backers, or investors in a shared project rather than rescuers meeting an emergency. DO NOT tag humble plea or emergency language; please, urgent, and crisis are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary explicitly frames the donor as a partner or co-builder.
rationale: Entrepreneurship labs, garden builds, and media-studio launches should score high; emergency hygiene kits and winter-coat drives should score low. This should be more common in growth-oriented periods than in acute-crisis quarters.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that root the request in a specific local setting, neighborhood, region, or place-based circumstance rather than an abstract classroom anywhere. DO NOT tag generic culture or equity words without place reference; identity, fairness, and belonging are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary points to a concrete locale or local condition.
rationale: Place-based ecology projects and neighborhood-history collections should score high; generic intervention kits and decontextualized supply requests should score low. This should vary by geography and by projects tied to local curriculum themes.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that emphasize portability, mobility, modularity, or flexible movement across spaces rather than fixed installation. DO NOT tag built-in or permanent infrastructure terms; stage, wall mount, and greenhouse are NOT applicable unless the resource is explicitly mobile or modular.
rationale: Rolling STEM carts and grab-and-go literacy kits should score high; greenhouse installations and fixed stage-lighting projects should score low. This should rise in shared-space schools and multipurpose-room contexts.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around reasoning, inquiry, experimentation, debugging, decision-making, or flexible problem-solving as transferable skill-building. DO NOT tag rote practice or remediation unless the essay emphasizes applied thinking.
rationale: This keeps the transferable-thinking lane visible even when the project is not framed as open-ended inquiry.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around programmable creation, automation, robotics, circuits, microcontrollers, or building systems that respond to code, but without explicit AI framing. DO NOT tag essays that explicitly center AI-generated content, chatbots, or AI-specific tools.
rationale: This is a second intentional non-AI adjacency tag that can identify teachers working in automation or programmable systems who may be open to future AI-related expansion.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as establishing physical, cognitive, or emotional conditions required before ordinary instruction can work. Treat this as a broad background readiness signal. DO NOT tag compliance, standards, enrichment, career, or general engagement language unless the essay centers on readiness conditions themselves.
rationale: Breakfast, sensory, clothing, and other readiness-condition projects should score high. Because the signal is intentionally broad, it should usually be treated as substrate/context rather than a fresh slice-specific insight.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around calm, regulation, sensory comfort, de-escalation, or emotional steadiness as a prerequisite for participation. Treat this as immediate regulation need, not SEL curriculum or generic comfortable-room atmosphere. DO NOT tag broad creativity, autonomy, choice, exploration, or safety-equipment language without a regulation/protection component.
rationale: Immediate regulation and de-escalation signal. Important but high-gravity; use as substrate unless a group-specific regulation mechanism is present.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that link materials to social-emotional skill-building, self-management, empathy, coping, or classroom relationship skills rather than academic mastery alone. DO NOT tag broad readiness or comfort vocabulary without SEL language; breakfast, warm clothing, and quiet are NOT applicable unless the term itself points to SEL development.
rationale: SEL curriculum kits and calm-space reflection tools should score high; breakfast carts and phonics intervention sets should score low. This should rise after 2020 as schools foreground mental health and SEL.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the ask as the start or continuation of an ongoing relationship with supporters rather than a one-off transaction. DO NOT tag simple thank-you closings or urgent-now terms; gratitude alone, asap, and replacement language are NOT applicable unless the term explicitly signals sustained relationship or shared journey.
rationale: Multi-phase garden builds and classroom publication programs should score high; one-off emergency restock requests should score low. This should be more common in recurring program launches than in short-term relief appeals.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame need through distance, small scale, isolation, transport constraints, or thin local access typical of dispersed settings. DO NOT tag urban crowding or generic underfunding terms; overcrowded, title i, and equity are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary specifically signals distance or dispersion.
rationale: Remote-school library access requests and travel-constrained science projects should score high; dense urban classroom crowding essays should score low. This should vary mainly by geography rather than by quarter, with mild spikes in winter-weather periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around shielding students from harm, hazard, exposure, or unsafe environments at the classroom or institutional level. DO NOT tag sensory-calming or SEL vocabulary unless the term points to protection from external harm; fidget, mindfulness, and cozy are NOT applicable on their own.
rationale: Air purifier requests, sanitation projects, and emergency-preparedness materials should score high; art-showcase and inquiry-kit essays should score low. This should spike around public-health periods and after school safety incidents.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that tie the request to a recurring season or school-calendar moment such as back to school, summer reading, holiday drives, testing season, or winter weather. DO NOT tag general urgency or milestone language without a seasonal anchor; urgent and showcase are NOT applicable unless the term names a calendar period.
rationale: Back-to-school supply lists, winter-clothing drives, and summer-reading projects should score high; evergreen library refreshes and year-round makerspace asks should score low. This should cycle predictably by quarter.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around planning, sustained attention, persistence, organization, executive function, routines, or task completion as durable self-management skills. DO NOT tag generic calm, comfort, sensory-regulation, or mental-health language unless the essay explicitly emphasizes executive-skill building rather than immediate relief or emotional steadiness.
rationale: This should catch durable self-management and work-habit language such as planning, sustained attention, persistence, and follow-through, rather than generic calm-corner or emotional-comfort framing alone.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms with tactile, auditory, visual, kinesthetic, or regulation-related perceptual vocabulary, regardless of whether a full story or explicit outcome chain is present. Boundary: unlike anecdote_scene_setting, this category does not require a vignette; unlike causal_specificity, it fires on perceptual language even without a usage-to-result chain. DO NOT tag generic engagement or aspiration words such as exciting, creativity, or confidence unless the term itself is perceptual.
rationale: Sensory-room materials, textured reading supports, and noise-reduction projects should score high; benchmark rubrics and generic aspiration essays should score low. This should rise with SEL and neurodiversity-related requests over time.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that justify the request with formal standards, benchmark language, evidence-based practice, or external research authority. DO NOT tag personal observation or scene-setting language; noticed, watched, and circle time are NOT applicable unless they accompany an explicit standards or research frame.
rationale: Standards-aligned literacy intervention requests and benchmark-driven math projects should score high; anecdotal read-aloud or cozy-corner essays should score low. This should rise in testing seasons and standards-heavy policy periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that emphasize avoiding embarrassment, exposure, awkwardness, or social self-consciousness so students can participate confidently. DO NOT tag generic mastery or academic-growth words; benchmark, proficiency, and intervention are NOT applicable unless the term points to social confidence or stigma reduction.
rationale: Hygiene closets, uniform assistance, and sensory seating designed to reduce visible difference should score high; phonics intervention sets and test-prep binders should score low. This should rise in care-closet periods and in requests about personal-use essentials.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that emphasize choice, self-direction, ownership, self-selection, or learner autonomy in how students engage with materials or tasks. DO NOT tag safety, regulation, containment, calm-space, or sensory language unless the essay explicitly centers student choice or self-direction rather than regulation.
rationale: Independent-reading choice and student-led projects should score high; calm-corner and sensory-tool requests should score low unless choice is the explicit mechanism.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that describe items kept by individual students for personal use rather than shared classroom resources. DO NOT tag shared stations or communal furniture; class set, makerspace, and library are NOT applicable unless the term explicitly signals individual ownership or take-home assignment. DO NOT tag regulation or sensory tools whose primary frame is calming rather than personal ownership.
rationale: Personal hygiene kits, take-home books, and own-copy workbook requests should score high; shared makerspace tables and whole-class library shelves should score low. This should rise in care-closet and take-home learning periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Tag terms that frame need through student voice belonging identity; require a contrastive rhetorical or contextual signal. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Voice, belonging, identity affirmation beyond material representation.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that narrow the request to a clearly named subgroup rather than the whole classroom population. DO NOT tag broad whole-class nouns or universal classroom references; classroom, readers, and scholars are NOT applicable unless the term explicitly marks a subgroup.
rationale: Newcomer-language access requests and autistic-student sensory projects should score high; general classroom library refreshes should score low. This should vary with enrollment shifts and periods of stronger subgroup-targeted funding language.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request as an interconnected set of items or a full system rather than a single keystone item. DO NOT tag lone flagship objects, or generic phrases like set or collection on their own; microscope, rug, or printer are NOT applicable unless the vocabulary makes clear the ask is a coordinated bundle or classroom-wide package.
rationale: Class-set literacy kits and full calm-corner packages should score high; single 3D-printer or one-rug requests should score low. This should stay stable, with modest rises in packaged curriculum and starter-kit projects.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that present the teacher primarily as protector, nurturer, comfort-provider, or day-to-day caregiver rather than program architect, curriculum planner, or technical implementer. DO NOT tag compliance, design-planning, STEM-program, or pilot language unless the vocabulary explicitly centers caregiving work.
rationale: Care-closet, comfort, and nurturing essays should score high; standards-aligned STEM pilots and program-design requests should not be pulled into caregiver framing.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that make the teacher's scarcity-management labor visible: stretching materials, improvising around gaps, buying personally, or patching systems with extra effort. DO NOT tag ordinary request nouns without workaround language; supplies, books, and devices are NOT applicable unless the essay highlights the workaround itself.
rationale: Teacher-funded basic supply requests and rotating-station stopgaps should score high; straightforward lab-kit purchases and competition travel requests should score low. This should be more visible in austerity periods and in essays from heavily under-resourced classrooms.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around CTE, pathway-specific tools, technical training, certifications, trade skills, or industry-standard preparation. DO NOT tag general STEM or enrichment language unless the essay is clearly pathway- or skill-credential-oriented.
rationale: This keeps traditional workforce-development language visible without collapsing the broader life-course skill concept into CTE only.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that describe patching, fixing, stabilizing, or repairing something already damaged, broken, depleted, or failing. Boundary: unlike episodic_disruption_recovery, this category is about the request type, not the temporal shape of the need. If the item is described as broken, damaged, or failing now, prefer this category; if the replacement is framed as routine upkeep or refresh, prefer maintenance_replacement. DO NOT tag general disruption or recovery words like pandemic or reopening unless the term itself names repair or patching.
rationale: Broken-device replacements and worn-furniture fixes should score high; robotics-club launches and new greenhouse builds should score low. This should rise in quarters following disruptions and in schools with aging equipment, but remain lower in expansion-heavy periods.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that name structural resource inequity, funding gaps, or systemic disadvantage as the context for the request. Boundary: unlike household_basic_needs_spillover, this category names institutional causes rather than direct home-to-school hardship transfer. DO NOT tag pantry, hygiene, laundry, or breakfast terms unless the vocabulary itself points to structural underfunding or inequity.
rationale: Title I classroom technology requests and equity-framed library access projects should score high; pantry snack carts and hygiene closets should score low. This should rise during equity-focused discourse and in years of stronger public attention to the digital divide.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that anchor the request in immediate urgency, present pressure, or near-term classroom need rather than long-horizon planning. DO NOT tag words that point mainly to long-run preparation or broad future readiness; career, college, and future-focused terms are NOT applicable unless paired with urgent present-tense pressure.
rationale: Winter-coat drives, replacement-supply requests, and short-notice intervention projects should score high; career-exploration labs and future-pathway projects should score low. This signal should peak in acute hardship quarters and around start-of-term shortages.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for agriculture food systems careers; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for business entrepreneurship careers; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for green jobs sustainability careers; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic technology, devices, coding, apps, robotics, or edtech unless AI is explicit or strongly central.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for health careers; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for media creative industries; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for public safety civic careers; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for robotics engineering careers; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: industry. Tag explicit career, industry, credential, or skill-preparation language for transportation logistics aviation; sparse but useful for funder matching. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, STEM, or career language unless the industry/skill area is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but funder-useful industry facet for workforce-development requests; useful for retrieval and reporting even if it does not surface as an NMF topic.

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete material/request terms that clearly name basic classroom supplies as the requested support or resource type. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Standard classroom basics such as paper, pencils, notebooks, markers, folders...

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete material/request terms that clearly name calm space regulation tools as the requested support or resource type. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Calm corners, fidgets, sensory tools, mindfulness materials.

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete material/request terms that clearly name clothing warmth basic needs as the requested support or resource type. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Personal clothing/warmth as participation/readiness barrier; exclude costumes...

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete material/request terms that clearly name organization or storage as the requested support/resource type. Treat as material topology, not by itself an interpretive insight. DO NOT tag broad essential-supply language, generic classroom management, or storage-adjacent terms unless the storage/organization object is explicit.
rationale: Bins, carts, shelves, labels, and organizers. Useful as a request/material descriptor; promote as insight only when a domain-specific implementation mechanism appears.

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete material/request terms that clearly name school food pantry replenishment as the requested support or resource type. DO NOT collapse hunger relief, nutrition instruction, cooking, and food systems unless the term explicitly belongs to this signal.
rationale: Material/restock signal for snacks, pantry, breakfast supplies; not the same ...

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete material/request terms that clearly name sensory neurodiversity support as the requested support or resource type. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Sensory, autism, neurodiversity, regulation, flexible seating.

description: Namespace: sensitive_context. Tag only explicit, direct, or affirming language for community violence safety context; do not infer from generic adjacent terms. DO NOT infer sensitive identity/context from generic inclusion, diversity, discipline, safety, behavior, or belonging language.
rationale: Sensitive-context signal for Justice-Adjacent Student Supports; should use direct or explicit language only and avoid proxy inference from generic adjacent terms.

description: Namespace: sensitive_context. Tag only explicit, direct, or affirming language for gang violence exposure context; do not infer from generic adjacent terms. DO NOT infer sensitive identity/context from generic inclusion, diversity, discipline, safety, behavior, or belonging language.
rationale: Sensitive-context signal for Justice-Adjacent Student Supports; should use direct or explicit language only and avoid proxy inference from generic adjacent terms.

description: Namespace: sensitive_context. Tag only explicit, direct, or affirming language for justice involved family context; do not infer from generic adjacent terms. DO NOT infer sensitive identity/context from generic inclusion, diversity, discipline, safety, behavior, or belonging language.
rationale: Sensitive-context signal for Justice-Adjacent Student Supports; should use direct or explicit language only and avoid proxy inference from generic adjacent terms.

description: Namespace: sensitive_context. Tag only explicit, direct, or affirming language for lgbtq affirming representation; do not infer from generic adjacent terms. DO NOT infer sensitive identity/context from generic inclusion, diversity, discipline, safety, behavior, or belonging language. Sparse management-priority exception: keep only direct or strongly explicit LGBTQ anchors; do not infer from generic diversity, inclusion, safety, belonging, or representation language.
rationale: Sensitive-context signal for LGBTQ; should use direct or explicit language only and avoid proxy inference from generic adjacent terms.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where AI-enabled accessibility or assistive use is explicit or strongly central, such as AI-supported translation, speech recognition, captioning, reading support, communication, or adaptive assistance. DO NOT tag generic assistive technology, tablets, devices, coding, apps, robotics, or edtech unless AI-enabled functionality is explicit.
rationale: AI-enabled accessibility subset, not generic assistive/adaptive technology. Keep sparse and high precision.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where AI literacy, generative AI, LLMs, prompting, machine learning, or AI-generated content is the instructional domain or tool domain being taught/used. DO NOT tag generic technology, devices, coding, robotics, algorithms, digital citizenship, privacy, apps, or edtech unless AI is explicit or strongly central.
rationale: Direct AI/LLM/tool literacy signal. Designed to avoid swallowing generic CS, digital citizenship, and device-access requests.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where assistive adaptive technology is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Assistive/adaptive tech; may include AI-enabled subset.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where climate environmental science learning is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Climate science, weather, ecology, environmental systems.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where coding computer science is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Coding, programming, CS; do not assume AI unless AI terms appear.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where cooking culinary life skills is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT collapse hunger relief, nutrition instruction, cooking, and food systems unless the term explicitly belongs to this signal.
rationale: Cooking may be health, life skill, or career pathway; route based on essay la...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where cte credentials certifications is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: CTE, certification, credentials, industry-recognized skills, OSHA, ServSafe, ...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where employability durable skills is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Teamwork, communication, problem solving, professionalism, leadership; avoid ...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where engineering design maker learning is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Engineering design, building, prototyping, makerspace.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where english language development is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit. Boundary: this is instructional English-language development, not home-language affirmation or translation/access logistics.
rationale: ELD/ESL instruction, vocabulary, language development.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where entrepreneurship business skills is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: School stores, business plans, marketing, entrepreneurship; useful for funders.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where financial literacy personal finance is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Budgeting, saving, banking, taxes, money management; may support workforce bu...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where food systems agriculture learning is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT collapse hunger relief, nutrition instruction, cooking, and food systems unless the term explicitly belongs to this signal.
rationale: Gardens, agriculture, composting, farm-to-table; avoid tagging pantry/restock...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where home language family literacy is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit. Boundary: this category is about home-language and family-literacy connection, not ELD instruction or translation logistics.
rationale: Home language, bilingual family literacy, caregiver engagement.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where math manipulatives quantitative learning is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Math manipulatives, measurement, quantitative reasoning; avoid broad math uni...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where multilingual language access is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Translation, bilingual access, multilingual participation logistics.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where nutrition literacy health education is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT collapse hunger relief, nutrition instruction, cooking, and food systems unless the term explicitly belongs to this signal.
rationale: Food as instructional content, not food as unmet basic need.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where phonics decodable reading intervention is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Decodables, phonics, fluency, intervention; may also crosswalk to remediation...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where restorative practices conflict resolution is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Restorative practices, mediation, conflict resolution, peer repair; likely mo...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where robotics makerspace 3d printing is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Robotics and maker technologies; may crosswalk to workforce depending career ...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where science inquiry lab learning is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Science labs, experiments, investigations; separate from general STEM.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag terms where sel curriculum skill building is the instructional domain, tool domain, or topic being taught/used. DO NOT tag broad DonorsChoose genre words or adjacent subject terms unless the specific signal is explicit.
rationale: Explicit SEL curriculum/skills: empathy, coping, self-awareness, relationship...

description: Namespace: subject. Tag direct use, teaching, or evaluation of explicit AI tools in classroom learning or instructional work. This is the high-precision AI anchor category. DO NOT tag generic coding, robotics, digital citizenship, device access, adaptive software, or edtech unless AI, generative AI, LLMs, chatbots, prompting, machine learning, or a named AI product is explicit.
rationale: Sparse but strategically important direct AI signal. Designed to recover explicit AI use without absorbing generic technology, coding, or device-access projects.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag student or teacher use of AI to create, draft, generate, design, edit, or produce writing, images, media, code, presentations, or other outputs. DO NOT tag general media production, graphic design, creative writing, or Canva-style production unless AI use is explicit.
rationale: Separates true AI-assisted creation from broader creative/media production and helps identify emerging generative-AI classroom use.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag AI-specific tutoring, coaching, personalization, feedback, or practice support. DO NOT tag generic adaptive software, personalized learning, intervention programs, or edtech platforms unless AI or a named AI tutoring/feedback tool is explicit.
rationale: Captures emerging AI tutoring and feedback use without conflating it with ordinary adaptive or intervention technology.

description: Namespace: context. Tag classroom policy, integrity, privacy, acceptable-use, or management concerns that explicitly involve AI tools or AI-generated work. DO NOT tag general academic integrity, plagiarism, privacy, phone management, digital citizenship, or locked storage unless AI is explicit.
rationale: Captures implementation and governance concerns around AI use, separate from teaching AI concepts or using AI for creation.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame technology as an operational continuity/reliability need: keeping digital instruction functional through replacement, charging, repair, software renewal, headphones, connectivity, or dependable equipment. Use when the continuity problem is specifically digital infrastructure reliability. Contrast with framing_chronic_scarcity, which is about recurring insufficiency across classroom supplies/materials generally; contrast with maintenance/repair categories when the technology dimension is not central. DO NOT tag technology as subject learning, coding, AI, robotics, makerspace, or enrichment unless the essay centers ongoing reliability or continuity.
rationale: Separates tech as operational backbone from tech as subject/domain learning. Useful for post-device-adoption continuity and replacement pressure.

description: Namespace: context. Tag explicit classroom or school management of student cell phones/personal mobile devices, including distraction, storage, restriction, phone-free routines, or phone policies. DO NOT tag school-issued tablets, Chromebooks, calculators, headphones, or assistive devices unless personal phones are explicit.
rationale: Current K-12 classroom-management signal for personal phones and distraction; distinct from school technology access or continuity.

description: Namespace: request. Tag concrete requested materials used to store, restrict, organize, or manage student cell phones/personal mobile devices. DO NOT tag generic storage, supply organizers, headphone storage, tablet carts, or charging carts unless personal phones are explicit.
rationale: Material/request companion to cellphone-management context. Keeps phone-specific tools from being lost in generic storage/organization.

description: Namespace: subject. Tag explicit special education/disability transition planning toward adulthood, independent living, employment, postsecondary participation, community routines, or functional life skills. DO NOT tag generic career readiness, future readiness, life skills, or independence unless disability, transition services, adulthood, or postsecondary planning is explicit.
rationale: Captures a distinct and funder-relevant K-12 transition-planning signal, especially for students with disabilities, without collapsing it into generic career/future framing.

description: Namespace: framing. Terms that frame the request around a concrete transition point into the next school, grade band, postsecondary setting, workplace, or adult-life stage. DO NOT tag generic future readiness, preparedness, career, or college language unless a specific transition point or next-stage move is named.
rationale: Concrete transition-stage framing. Keeps specific transitions visible without broadening future-readiness tautology.

description: Namespace: context. Tag direct fiscal or budget-pressure language tied to ESSER, expiring pandemic relief funds, budget cuts, program cuts, loss of temporary funds, or post-relief sustainability pressure. DO NOT tag generic underfunding, low-income context, scarcity, Title I, or not-enough-supplies language unless budget cuts, expiring funds, or named relief funding are explicit.
rationale: Sparse but strategically important fiscal context. Captures ESSER-cliff and budget-cycle pressure distinct from chronic scarcity.

description: Namespace: context. Tag direct student mental-health, anxiety, stress, grief, trauma, or wellbeing context when named as a condition, barrier, or intervention context. This is a context signal, not a request/material signal: a project may also receive a regulation-tool tag when it explicitly names both anxiety/stress/trauma and calm-corner/sensory materials. DO NOT tag calm corners, fidgets, flexible seating, mindfulness, or SEL materials unless direct mental-health/anxiety/stress/grief/trauma context is explicit. DO NOT infer mental health from generic dysregulation, focus, behavior, comfort, or classroom climate language alone.
rationale: Separates direct mental-health context from generic regulation tools and SEL curriculum, reducing overinterpretation of calm-space requests.

description: Namespace: context. Tag operational school disruption or student health/access needs tied to climate, extreme heat, wildfire smoke, air quality, cooling, asthma triggers, or weather-related environmental conditions. DO NOT tag environmental science, gardens, sustainability, or climate lessons unless the essay centers operational disruption, health, or access conditions.
rationale: Captures climate/environment as school operating condition rather than instructional subject matter.

description: Namespace: context. Tag explicit teacher staffing, substitute shortage, vacancy, understaffing, coverage, or long-term substitute context affecting classroom operation or student support. DO NOT tag generic teacher support, small group support, aides, or classroom help unless staffing shortage or vacancy is explicit.
rationale: Sparse current K-12 context signal. Useful when teachers explicitly connect requests to staffing or substitute-coverage pressure.

description: Namespace: context. Tag explicit chronic absenteeism, attendance recovery, school refusal, return-to-school, or reengagement after missed school. DO NOT infer from generic engagement, motivation, belonging, participation, or hardship unless attendance/absence/reengagement is explicit or mechanically tied.
rationale: Sharper current attendance/reengagement signal that distinguishes chronic absence and return-to-school strategies from generic engagement.

description: Namespace: context. Tag explicit school physical-safety protocols, emergency preparedness, lockdowns, evacuation, crisis response, first aid, or security routines. DO NOT tag lab safety, safety goggles, emotional safety, safe spaces, or general protection language unless school safety/security protocol is explicit.
rationale: Separates physical school-safety/security protocols from broad safety, regulation, sanitation, and lab-safety language.
